threats.title: Relayer Gas Griefing to Steal Message Processing Fees
threats.hypothesis_description: A malicious relayer can call processMessage with barely enough gas for the bridge overhead but insufficient gas for the actual message execution. The EVM's 63/64 rule means the inner call receives less gas than _message.gasLimit. The call fails, the message is marked RETRIABLE, but the relayer still receives _message.fee. The user's message is stuck in RETRIABLE, requiring the destOwner to retry without any fee compensation.
threats.investigation_rationale: processMessage does not verify gasleft() >= _message.gasLimit before forwarding to _invokeMessageCall. ExcessivelySafeCall silently uses whatever gas is available, making under-gas attacks undetectable by the bridge logic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: gasLimit = _message.gasLimit used without verifying gasleft() >= gasLimit; ExcessivelySafeCall forwards min(gasLimit, 63/64*gasleft)

preconditions: Message has non-zero fee (incentive for relayer)
msg.sender != _message.destOwner (uses _message.gasLimit not gasleft())
On L2: invocationDelay == 0 so anyone can immediately process
On L1: attacker is preferredExecutor or waits extra delay

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Relayer submits processMessage with total gas = bridge_overhead + small_buffer, far below _message.gasLimit
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - fully exploitable by any on-chain actor
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Available for every message with fee > 0 processed by a non-destOwner

operational_questions: What is the minimum gas overhead for processMessage before _invokeMessageCall?
How much profit can a relayer extract per griefed message?
Is there monitoring for RETRIABLE messages that should have succeeded?
validation_requirements: Calculate exact gas overhead for processMessage (proof verification, storage reads/writes)
Test with gasLimit set to bridge_overhead + 100 to confirm inner call fails
Verify fee is paid even when message becomes RETRIABLE

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor for sent bridge messages with profitable fees
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Submit processMessage with just enough gas for bridge overhead (~200k gas) but far less than _message.gasLimit
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Inner call fails due to insufficient gas, message marked RETRIABLE
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Collect _message.fee as msg.sender while user's execution didn't happen

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users lose their processing fees and messages stuck in RETRIABLE state
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: destOwner must call retryMessage (no fee) or processMessage with adequate gas
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All bridge message processing with non-zero fees

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic - calculate minimum gas for bridge overhead
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time a message with fee is ready to process

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Messages transitioning to RETRIABLE despite adequate message gasLimit
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Relayer addresses consistently processing messages that become RETRIABLE
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Same relayer profiting from fees on many RETRIABLE messages

threats.title: changeBridgedToken Deletes Wrong Mapping Key - Stale bridgedToCanonical Entry
threats.hypothesis_description: In ERC20Vault.changeBridgedToken, `delete bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew]` is a no-op because the validation above already confirmed _btokenNew has no existing mapping. The intended `delete bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_]` is never executed. After token migration, the old bridged token retains its canonical mapping in storage, breaking the bijection between bridgedToCanonical and canonicalToBridged. Multiple stale entries accumulate across successive migrations.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a confirmed code bug where line 179 deletes the wrong key. The stale mapping is currently guarded by the btokenBlacklist check in sendToken, but the storage inconsistency creates risk for future upgrades or off-chain indexers.

proof_of_signal.pattern: delete bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew] is no-op; should be delete bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_]

preconditions: Owner calls changeBridgedToken with a valid old token existing
btokenOld_ != address(0) (there is an existing bridged token to replace)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: After changeBridgedToken: bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_].addr != address(0) despite btokenOld_ being replaced
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Triggered automatically by any owner-initiated token migration
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Every token migration leaves stale mapping data permanently

operational_questions: Are there any code paths that check bridgedToCanonical without also checking btokenBlacklist?
Could a future upgrade introduce a path that treats the stale mapping as valid?
Do off-chain indexers rely on bridgedToCanonical for token resolution?
validation_requirements: Verify bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] persists after changeBridgedToken call
Confirm the deletion on line 179 is indeed a no-op
Test successive migrations to confirm stale entries accumulate

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Observe that after any token migration, bridgedToCanonical has stale entry
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: In current code, blacklist prevents exploitation via sendToken
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If contract is upgraded and new logic queries bridgedToCanonical without blacklist check, stale entry becomes exploitable
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Old token would be treated as valid bridged token, potentially allowing double-counting
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires contract upgrade to introduce exploitable code path (latent vulnerability)

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Storage inconsistency in vault mapping state
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Off-chain indexers may show incorrect token relationships
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to clean stale entries

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Occurs on every changeBridgedToken call with existing old token

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Multiple bridgedToCanonical entries mapping to same canonical token

threats.title: Permanent Message DoS via Reverting refundTo Address
threats.hypothesis_description: If _message.refundTo is set to a contract that cannot receive ETH (reverts on receive/fallback), processMessage always reverts at the refundTo.sendEther() call. The message is permanently stuck in NEW status. Since it never reaches FAILED on the destination chain, recallMessage on the source chain cannot be triggered. The message's value and fee, plus any tokens locked in the source vault, are permanently frozen.
threats.investigation_rationale: LibAddress.sendEther uses ExcessivelySafeCall which returns success=false if the target reverts, then LibAddress reverts with ETH_TRANSFER_FAILED. This reverts the entire processMessage. Even when refundAmount is 0, sendEther(0) still makes a call to refundTo which can revert.

proof_of_signal.pattern: refundTo.sendEther(refundAmount) reverts if refundTo is a contract that rejects ETH; same issue at srcOwner.sendEther in recallMessage line 174

preconditions: refundTo address is a contract that reverts on ETH receive (or srcOwner for recall path)
Message is in NEW status on destination chain
No alternative processing path exists to bypass the sendEther call

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: processMessage always reverts for specific messages despite valid proofs
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - can affect any user who sets refundTo to a non-receiving contract
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent - no timeout or admin rescue mechanism for stuck messages

operational_questions: Can destOwner process the message (as msg.sender == refundTo path)?
If message.value == 0 and refundAmount == 0, does sendEther(0) still revert?
Is there any admin function to force-complete stuck messages?
validation_requirements: Deploy reverting contract as refundTo, send bridge message, confirm processMessage permanently fails
Verify sendEther(address, 0) still makes a call that can revert
Confirm no rescue path exists for permanently stuck messages

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User sets refundTo to a proxy wallet that later upgrades to reject ETH
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Bridge message becomes unprocessable on destination chain
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Source chain tokens/ETH permanently locked since recall requires FAILED status
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: No admin intervention possible - no force-complete function exists
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None for self-griefing; proxy upgrade for weaponized version

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent loss of bridged assets for affected messages
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires protocol upgrade to add rescue mechanism
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Any bridge message with reverting refundTo/srcOwner

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - single message with reverting refundTo
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Low - deploy a contract without receive/fallback
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: refundTo must be reverting when processMessage is called

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Messages stuck in NEW status with repeated failed processMessage attempts
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: refundTo addresses that are contracts without receive functions

threats.title: SignalService Cannot Be Paused - No Emergency Brake for Proof Verification
threats.hypothesis_description: SignalService._authorizePause always reverts with SS_UNSUPPORTED, making the contract permanently unpausable. If a vulnerability is found in Merkle proof verification (LibTrieProof, MerkleTrie, or the multi-hop logic), there is no way to halt proof processing. While the Bridge can be paused, SignalService.proveSignalReceived remains callable by anyone, and any cached chain data from proof verification persists.
threats.investigation_rationale: The inherited pause() and unpause() functions from EssentialContract are publicly callable but always revert for SignalService. This is intentional but creates a gap in emergency response capability.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function _authorizePause(address) internal pure override { revert SS_UNSUPPORTED(); }

preconditions: A vulnerability exists in proof verification logic
Bridge pausing alone is insufficient because SignalService operations continue independently

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: SignalService.pause() always reverts - dead code path
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent - no pause capability by design

operational_questions: Can pausing the Bridge alone prevent all exploitation of a SignalService vulnerability?
Can proveSignalReceived be called directly to cache malicious data even while Bridge is paused?
Is there an alternative emergency mechanism (e.g., deauthorizing all addresses)?
validation_requirements: Confirm SignalService.pause() always reverts
Test if proveSignalReceived with caching options can write to storage when called directly
Verify if owner can deauthorize all syncChainData callers as alternative emergency brake

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify vulnerability in MerkleTrie/LibTrieProof proof verification
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Exploit vulnerability to forge proofs while Bridge is paused but SignalService is active
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call proveSignalReceived directly with CACHE_STATE_ROOT to write malicious roots
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: When Bridge is unpaused, process messages using cached malicious roots
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires finding a Merkle proof verification bug

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Loss of emergency response capability for cross-chain verification
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Cannot halt proof verification during active exploit
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to add pause capability

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: High - requires first finding a proof verification vulnerability

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Unexpected chain data syncs during Bridge pause periods
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: proveSignalReceived calls with unusual caching options
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Direct calls to proveSignalReceived outside normal bridge flow

threats.title: Fee-On-Transfer Token Drain on Destination Chain ERC20Vault
threats.hypothesis_description: ERC20Vault._handleMessage correctly measures balanceChange via before/after balanceOf on the source chain for fee-on-transfer tokens. However, _transferTokens on the destination chain calls IERC20(token_).safeTransfer(_to, _amount) with the full recorded amount. If the canonical token on the destination chain is a fee-on-transfer token, the vault transfers _amount from its balance but the recipient receives less. Over many bridge cycles, the vault's reserves are drained faster than bridged tokens are returned, eventually making the vault insolvent.
threats.investigation_rationale: Source-side accounting is correct (balance delta), but destination-side does not re-measure the actual transfer amount, creating an asymmetry that accumulates over repeated operations.
deployment_assumptions: Fee-on-transfer ERC20 token is used as a canonical token on the destination chain
threats.config_evidence: ERC20Vault.sol:_transferTokens line uses safeTransfer without post-transfer balance check; _handleMessage on source uses balance delta

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(token_).safeTransfer(_to, _amount) transfers full _amount without measuring actual received amount on destination

preconditions: Canonical token on destination chain has transfer fees
Token is bridged back to origin chain where it is canonical
Multiple round-trip bridge operations occur

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Destination vault's canonical token balance decreases faster than expected with each bridge operation
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - occurs naturally with fee-on-transfer tokens
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Accumulates over time with each bridge operation

operational_questions: Which canonical tokens in the vault have transfer fees?
How quickly does the drain accumulate relative to bridging volume?
Is there monitoring for vault balance vs bridged token total supply?
validation_requirements: Deploy fee-on-transfer token, perform round-trip bridging, measure vault balance divergence
Calculate break-even point where vault becomes insolvent
Verify safeTransfer does not account for fee

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify fee-on-transfer canonical token held by vault
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Repeatedly bridge tokens to destination and back
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Each round trip reduces vault's actual token balance by transfer fee amount
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Eventually vault cannot fulfill withdrawal requests, last users lose funds
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Multiple bridge operations required; can be accelerated with flash loans

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires injecting tokens to make vault whole
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Token bridging for affected canonical tokens

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Vault balance diverging from expected amount based on bridged supply
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users unable to withdraw full amounts from vault
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated bridge round-trips for specific tokens

threats.title: Unchecked Overflow in invocationDelay + invocationExtraDelay Could Bypass Timing Security
threats.hypothesis_description: In processMessage, `unchecked { invocationDelay += invocationExtraDelay; }` wraps around on overflow. While current hardcoded values in getInvocationDelays are safe, getInvocationDelays is marked `virtual`, allowing derived contracts to return large values. If a derived Bridge contract returns values summing above type(uint256).max, the delay wraps to a small value, allowing non-preferred executors to process messages almost immediately, bypassing the timing security window.
threats.investigation_rationale: The unchecked arithmetic on security-critical delay values is dangerous when the function providing those values is overridable. A malicious or buggy override could exploit the overflow.
deployment_assumptions: A derived Bridge contract overrides getInvocationDelays with values that overflow when summed
threats.config_evidence: Bridge.sol getInvocationDelays is virtual; current hardcoded values are safe but overridability creates risk

proof_of_signal.pattern: unchecked { invocationDelay += invocationExtraDelay; } on security-critical timing values

preconditions: Bridge is deployed as derived contract with overridden getInvocationDelays
Sum of invocationDelay + invocationExtraDelay exceeds type(uint256).max
Non-preferred executor attempts to process message

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Non-preferred executors processing messages without expected extra delay
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires deployment of derived contract with buggy override
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After proof but during intended delay period

operational_questions: Are there any deployed derived Bridge contracts overriding getInvocationDelays?
Could a governance proposal introduce a bad override?
Is there input validation on the returned delay values?
validation_requirements: Create derived Bridge with large delay values that overflow
Confirm wrapped delay allows immediate execution
Verify current production Bridge does not override getInvocationDelays

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Propose upgrade to Bridge with overridden getInvocationDelays returning near-max values
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: After upgrade, non-preferred executors' delay wraps to near-zero
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Process messages immediately without the intended security delay
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Execute malicious messages before watchdog can react
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires governance control or malicious upgrade

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Timing security bypassed for non-preferred executors
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to add checked arithmetic

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Enough for governance proposal if applicable

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Messages processed faster than expected on L1
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Non-preferred executors processing immediately after proof

threats.title: Suspend-then-Unsuspend Enables Processing Fabricated Bridge Messages Without Proof
threats.hypothesis_description: The bridge_watchdog (or owner) can call suspendMessages(hashes, true) then suspendMessages(hashes, false) for ANY arbitrary msgHash. Unsuspending sets proofReceipt[msgHash].receivedAt = block.timestamp. In processMessage, isMessageProven = (receivedAt != 0) which is now true, so _proveSignalReceived is completely skipped. Since messageStatus defaults to NEW (0) for all unused hashes, a crafted message matching the hash can be processed without any Merkle proof. Unlike recallMessage which additionally checks isSignalSent, processMessage has no such secondary verification.
threats.investigation_rationale: The asymmetry between processMessage (no isSignalSent check) and recallMessage (has isSignalSent check) means processMessage is uniquely vulnerable to the suspend/unsuspend bypass. A compromised watchdog could enable arbitrary message execution on the destination chain.

proof_of_signal.pattern: suspendMessages sets proofReceipt[msgHash].receivedAt = block.timestamp on unsuspend; processMessage skips proof if receivedAt != 0; no isSignalSent check in processMessage unlike recallMessage

preconditions: bridge_watchdog key is compromised (or owner key)
Attacker can compute hashMessage for a crafted message
Destination chain is the one where processMessage is called
On L1: must wait invocationDelay (1h) + invocationExtraDelay (384s) after unsuspend

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: suspendMessages called for hashes that were never proven, followed by unsuspend, then processMessage
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires bridge_watchdog or owner key compromise
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After unsuspend + invocationDelay on L1; immediate on L2 (zero delay)

operational_questions: Is the bridge_watchdog address a multisig with adequate threshold?
Are suspendMessages calls monitored for hashes that lack corresponding sent messages?
Can the suspend/unsuspend be made proof-aware (require prior proof before unsuspend restores processability)?
validation_requirements: Craft arbitrary message, compute hash, suspend+unsuspend, confirm processMessage succeeds without proof
Verify that recallMessage is protected by isSignalSent check but processMessage is not
Quantify maximum extractable value via fabricated messages

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Craft message targeting ERC20Vault.onMessageInvocation with arbitrary canonical token drain data
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Compute msgHash, call suspendMessages([msgHash], true) then suspendMessages([msgHash], false)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Wait invocationDelay, call processMessage with crafted message - proof is skipped

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete bridge drain possible - any message can be fabricated
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All vault tokens and bridge ETH can be stolen
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires emergency response, potential hard fork consideration

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Medium - need watchdog key + craft valid message encoding
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Advanced - message construction matching vault ABI
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: On L2: immediate; On L1: 1h+ after unsuspend

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: suspendMessages calls followed by unsuspend for non-proven hashes
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: processMessage succeeding without prior proof receipt from legitimate sources
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Watchdog address calling suspend/unsuspend in rapid succession

threats.title: Banning Vault Address Causes Permanent Token Loss for In-Flight Messages
threats.hypothesis_description: If the bridge_watchdog bans a vault address (e.g., erc20_vault on destination chain), all in-flight messages targeting that vault are processed with status DONE immediately, with _message.value refunded but no actual token transfer executed. The message data (containing token transfer instructions) is silently discarded. Since the status is DONE (not FAILED), recallMessage on the source chain cannot be triggered. The tokens locked/burned on the source chain are permanently lost.
threats.investigation_rationale: processMessage handles banned target addresses by setting refundAmount = _message.value and status = DONE without invoking the message. The token payload in _message.data is never executed, and DONE status prevents recall.

proof_of_signal.pattern: addressBanned[_message.to] causes refundAmount = _message.value and status = DONE; token data in message.data ignored; DONE prevents recall

preconditions: Watchdog or owner bans a vault address
In-flight messages exist targeting that vault
Messages are processed after ban takes effect

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Vault address banned → in-flight messages processed as DONE without token delivery → tokens permanently locked on source
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires bridge_watchdog or owner to ban the vault address
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: While vault address is banned and in-flight messages are being processed

operational_questions: Are there operational procedures to delay banning vault addresses?
Could a two-step ban with time delay allow in-flight messages to be processed first?
Is there a mechanism to recover tokens from permanently DONE messages?
validation_requirements: Ban vault address, process in-flight message, confirm tokens are lost
Verify DONE status prevents recallMessage
Confirm no admin function to reset DONE messages

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromised watchdog identifies vault address on destination chain
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Bans vault address while high-value messages are in-flight
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Relayers process messages → DONE without token delivery
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Source chain tokens permanently locked (ERC20/721/1155 in vault, burned bridged tokens unrecoverable)
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: bridge_watchdog key compromise + timing with in-flight messages

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Targeted token theft via admin action
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All in-flight messages to banned address lose their token payloads
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No recovery mechanism - requires protocol upgrade
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All token vault operations for banned vault

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Medium - must time ban with in-flight messages
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: During active bridging with high-value in-flight messages

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Ban events for vault addresses; DONE messages with no corresponding vault events
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Messages marked DONE without TokenReceived events
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Vault addresses being banned and quickly unbanned

threats.title: Bridge Watchdog Can Indefinitely Freeze Funds With No Escape Mechanism
threats.hypothesis_description: The bridge_watchdog can call suspendMessages for any message hashes, setting receivedAt = type(uint64).max. Suspended messages cannot be processed because block.timestamp < invocationDelay + type(uint64).max is always true. There is no timeout mechanism, no escape hatch, and no way for affected users to recover their funds. The watchdog can indefinitely freeze any number of messages without any governance override.
threats.investigation_rationale: The suspension mechanism has no time limit and no governance or owner override to force-unsuspend. Once suspended, only the watchdog (or owner) can unsuspend, creating a single point of failure for fund recovery.

proof_of_signal.pattern: proofReceipt[msgHash].receivedAt = type(uint64).max; no timeout or governance override for unsuspension

preconditions: bridge_watchdog or owner key is compromised or acts maliciously
Messages are suspended and never unsuspended
No alternative governance mechanism can force unsuspend

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Messages suspended with no subsequent unsuspend; funds frozen indefinitely
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires watchdog or owner to suspend and refuse to unsuspend

operational_questions: Is there a governance process to replace the bridge_watchdog address?
Can the owner override a malicious watchdog's suspensions?
Should a maximum suspension duration be implemented?
validation_requirements: Confirm owner can also call suspendMessages to unsuspend
Verify no alternative unsuspend path exists
Test if AddressManager change to bridge_watchdog affects existing suspensions

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromise bridge_watchdog key or act as malicious watchdog
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Suspend all high-value in-flight message hashes
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Refuse to unsuspend, holding funds hostage
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Demand ransom or cause protocol-wide disruption

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Targeted or mass fund freezing with no automatic recovery
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires owner intervention via onlyFromOwnerOrNamed modifier; if owner is timelock, requires governance vote
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All bridge messages can be targeted

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: 0 (for watchdog); governance proposal needed if owner is timelock
execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - single transaction per message batch

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large batches of suspended messages without subsequent unsuspend
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Suspension events for valid, proven messages
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Watchdog suspending without corresponding threat indicators

threats.title: Bridge Message Selector Bypass for Contracts with Short Data Payloads
threats.hypothesis_description: _invokeMessageCall checks if data.length >= 4 AND selector != onMessageInvocation AND target.isContract() → sets success_ = false. However, if data.length < 4 (1-3 bytes), the check is bypassed entirely and ExcessivelySafeCall is made to any contract target with arbitrary short data. This invokes the contract's fallback function with attacker-controlled short data. While impact is limited by what fallback functions do, it allows arbitrary fallback invocation on any non-banned contract through the bridge with ETH value.
threats.investigation_rationale: The selector filter intends to restrict bridge calls to only onMessageInvocation, but the length check creates a bypass for short payloads that trigger fallback functions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (_message.data.length >= 4 ...) check allows data.length 1-3 to bypass selector restriction; excessivelySafeCall proceeds with short data to contract

preconditions: Message data is 1-3 bytes long
Target is a contract address
Target is not banned, not address(0), not bridge, not signal service
Message has valid proof (or proof bypass via suspend/unsuspend)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Bridge messages with 1-3 byte data invoking contract fallback functions
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - any user can craft such a message via sendMessage
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time - requires sending message with short data from source chain

operational_questions: Are there any contracts addressable through the bridge whose fallback functions have security-relevant behavior?
Can ETH value combined with short data trigger unexpected behavior in protocol contracts?
Is there a legitimate use case for 1-3 byte data messages?
validation_requirements: Send bridge message with 3-byte data to contract, confirm fallback is invoked
Survey protocol contracts for fallback function behavior
Verify if empty data (length 0) is also allowed (yes, it is - for ETH transfers)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify target contract with exploitable fallback function
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Craft bridge message with 1-3 byte data and ETH value targeting that contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Send message from source chain, process on destination
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Fallback function invoked with ETH value and short data

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Depends on target contract's fallback behavior
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Limited - most fallback functions are benign or simply receive ETH
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - unlikely to cause permanent damage

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Low - craft message with short data

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Bridge messages with data length 1-3 bytes to contract targets
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected fallback invocations on protocol contracts
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Messages specifically crafted to bypass selector check

threats.title: Missing bridge_watchdog and bridge_pauser Registration in Deployment Creates Operational Gap
threats.hypothesis_description: The deployment scripts (DeployOnL1.s.sol) do not register 'bridge_watchdog' or 'bridge_pauser' named addresses in the AddressManager. Since Bridge uses onlyFromOwnerOrNamed('bridge_watchdog') and _authorizePause checks onlyFromOwnerOrNamed('bridge_pauser'), these resolve to address(0) when not registered. The modifiers allow owner OR the named address, so only the owner (timelock) can perform these actions. This means watchdog and pause operations require governance proposals through the timelock, adding significant latency (7 days per the timelock deployment) to emergency responses.
threats.investigation_rationale: The deployment script registers signal_service, bridge, vaults, and bridged token implementations but never registers bridge_watchdog or bridge_pauser. The 7-day timelock on owner actions creates a dangerous gap in emergency response capability.
deployment_assumptions: bridge_watchdog and bridge_pauser are not registered post-deployment via separate transactions
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol deploySharedContracts function registers signal_service, bridge, erc20_vault, erc721_vault, erc1155_vault, bridged_erc20, bridged_erc721, bridged_erc1155 but never registers bridge_watchdog or bridge_pauser

proof_of_signal.pattern: No register() calls for 'bridge_watchdog' or 'bridge_pauser' in deployment script; owner set to timelock with 7-day delay

preconditions: bridge_watchdog and bridge_pauser not registered in AddressManager after deployment
Owner is timelock with 7-day delay
Emergency situation requires rapid pause or message suspension

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Emergency bridge operations require 7-day timelock governance process
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Only timelock (owner) can perform watchdog/pause functions without named address registration
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: 7-day window where emergency response is delayed

operational_questions: Are bridge_watchdog and bridge_pauser registered in a separate post-deployment step?
What is the actual timelock delay configured in production?
Is there a security council fast-path for emergency timelock operations?
validation_requirements: Query AddressManager for bridge_watchdog and bridge_pauser registrations
Verify timelock delay for owner operations
Check if security council has direct proposer+executor roles on timelock

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify that watchdog/pauser addresses are not registered
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Exploit a bridge vulnerability knowing emergency response takes 7 days via timelock
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Execute attack within the 7-day response window
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Drain bridge assets before suspension can take effect
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires finding an exploitable vulnerability first

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Delayed emergency response by governance timelock duration
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Inability to quickly pause bridge or suspend suspicious messages
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - register watchdog/pauser addresses via governance

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - operational gap exists by default
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time before watchdog/pauser are registered

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: AddressManager queries for bridge_watchdog and bridge_pauser returning address(0)
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Owner (timelock) being the only address that can pause/suspend
